title: Prepared Statements and Binding Parameters
description: Protect against SQL injection with prepared statements.

slides.title: SQL Injection Danger
slides.content:
# Never Trust User Input

If you insert user input directly into SQL, attackers can manipulate your query. Example:

```php
// Dangerous!
$sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '" . $_POST['name'] . "'";
```

If the user enters `' OR '1'='1`, the query becomes:
```sql
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '' OR '1'='1'
```
Which returns all users!

**Prepared statements** prevent this by separating SQL logic from data.

slides.title: Prepared Statements with PDO
Positional vs Named Placeholders
slides.codeSnippet:
<?php
$stmt = $pdo->prepare('SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > :age AND city = :city');
$stmt->execute(['age' => 18, 'city' => 'Paris']);
?>

slides.title: Benefits
slides.content:
## Why Prepared Statements?

*   **Security**: Input values are never concatenated into the query; they are sent separately, so injection is impossible.
*   **Performance**: For repeated queries, prepare once and execute many times.
*   **Clarity**: Code is cleaner and easier to read.

quizData.question: Which method sends the query with placeholders to the database?
quizData.explanation: prepare() sends the query template; execute() sends the values.
